selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Look, I don't have social media accounts myself, so maybe I see things differently than most people. I run my own small restaurant here in Lecce, and I've watched customers sitting at my tables scrolling through their phones, getting worked up about things that later turn out to be completely false. It's like watching people argue about a football match that never actually happened. From my perspective, the simplest solution is what I've done, just stay off these platforms entirely. I have three kids, and I tell them the same thing. Why put yourself in a position where you're constantly trying to figure out what's real and what's invented? Life is complicated enough without that extra burden. When I want real information, I talk to actual people. In my restaurant, I hear genuine stories and real conversations. That's worth more than a thousand posts online. But I understand not everyone wants to disconnect completely. My partner uses social media to keep in touch with family, and I see how it pulls her in different directions. What I've observed is that people need to treat online content the way I treat fish at the market. You look carefully, you smell it, you check the source. If something seems off, you don't buy it. The problem is most people don't apply that same practical judgment online. They see something that confirms what they already believe or makes them angry, and they share it without thinking. That's lazy, frankly. I'm quite particular about how I run my business, and I think people should be equally careful about what information they consume and spread. The platforms themselves should do more, certainly, but waiting for them to fix everything is like waiting for the government to solve all your problems. It won't happen. Personal responsibility matters. Check where information comes from before you believe it or pass it along. Ask yourself if it makes sense. Talk to people face to face when you can. And honestly, consider whether you really need to be on these platforms at all. I've managed fine without them, focusing instead on my work, my family, cycling through the Salento countryside, and painting when I have time. Real life doesn't need a filter or fact-checking algorithm.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 41-year-old self-employed restaurant owner in Lecce with primary school education who notably has no social media presence. This is the most critical factor. His high conscientiousness suggests he would approach topics methodically and emphasize responsibility. Low agreeableness indicates he would be direct, possibly blunt, and not overly concerned with softening his opinions. Low extraversion means he's more reserved and observant rather than socially engaged. His creative nature and hobbies suggest practical, hands-on thinking. His occupation gives him a unique vantage point observing others' social media behavior without participating himself. The tone should be straightforward, somewhat skeptical, grounded in practical experience, and unapologetic about his choices. He would likely emphasize personal responsibility over systemic solutions, given his self-employed status and low agreeableness. His lack of formal education but practical business experience would manifest in common-sense analogies rather than technical explanations.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, practical, somewhat skeptical, observational, unapologetic, grounded in real-world experience, straightforward without being aggressive, matter-of-fact
